original_hypothesis.title: LibAddress.isValidSignature may be misused with non-EIP1271 contracts leading to false negatives/DoS in auth checks
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: LibAddress.isValidSignature treats any contract address as EIP-1271 and calls isValidSignature. Contracts that are not wallets (and do not implement EIP-1271) will revert, causing the helper to revert and potentially DoS any authorization flow using it. While not used in the shown in-scope contracts directly, this library is part of the scope and could be used by other protocol components; its current behavior is not 'try/catch' wrapped.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If used in access control or message validation, a malicious actor could supply a contract address that reverts on isValidSignature and block execution paths. Practicality depends on usage elsewhere in repo; include as a hypothesis for Phase D call-site tracing.
deployment_assumptions: Some in-protocol authentication uses LibAddress.isValidSignature on user-supplied addresses/hashes (not shown in this snippet)
The address supplied can be a contract that does not implement IERC1271 correctly
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No direct call sites shown in provided scope excerpt; requires Phase D repo-wide search for LibAddress.isValidSignature usage.

proof_of_signal.lines: isValidSignature(): direct IERC1271(_addr).isValidSignature call without try/catch
proof_of_signal.pattern: Contract-path signature validation can revert and bubble up

preconditions: A state-changing function uses LibAddress.isValidSignature for authorization or validation
Attacker can choose _addr to be a reverting/non-1271 contract
No upstream try/catch handles the revert

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External call to arbitrary contract without error handling
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: DoS by supplying reverting contracts for signature checks
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try/catch around IERC1271 call; no supportsInterface check for IERC1271

analysis_questions: Where is LibAddress.isValidSignature used across the full protocol?
Is the checked address attacker-controlled in those call sites?
Would reverting behavior block critical operations (withdrawal, proof submission, bridging)?
validation_requirements: Repo-wide grep for isValidSignature usage and classify trust of inputs
Add tests with a contract that reverts on isValidSignature and ensure callers handle it
Consider adding try/catch or ERC165 check at call sites if needed

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker provides a contract address as signer/wallet in a flow that validates signatures via LibAddress.isValidSignature; the contract reverts, causing the entire transaction to revert and blocking the operation for that user or globally if used in shared paths.
prerequisites: Existence of a call site where attacker controls _addr

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Medium via liveness disruption of affected function(s)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users interacting with the affected auth flow
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Mitigate by try/catch and explicit interface checks

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing negative tests for non-1271 contract signers.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Track reverts originating from IERC1271.isValidSignature calls

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
